content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 7, 2020

Steve Mattas
City Attorney
City of Walnut Creek
555 12th Street, Suite 1500
Oakland, California 94607

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-235

Dear Mr. Mattas:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor Pro Tem Loella Haskew
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note
that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under
other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section
1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

Under the Act, may the Mayor Pro Tem of Walnut Creek take part in the City Council's
action(s) related to negotiation and approval of a lease of city-owned retail property, given that her
spouse receives income from the executive vice president of the company the City has contracted
with to act as its leasing agent?

CONCLUSION

No, under the Act, the Mayor has a disqualifying conflict of interest and may not take part in
the governmental decisions, as she has a source of income interest in the executive vice president,
who in turn has a business entity interest in the company, which will be materially financially
affected by the decisions, given that the company's compensation is directly tied to the leasing
decisions.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-235
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City of Walnut Creek ("City") owns a retail building with outside dining space on
Locust Street. In 2009, the City entered a ten-year lease with the restaurant Tender Greens for the
retail space in the southern portion of the building ("Property"). In July 2019, Tender Greens
requested, and the City agreed, to terminate the lease with Tender Greens and the Property became
vacant.

City staff issued a Request for Qualifications requesting proposals from commercial real
estate brokers who could assist the City in soliciting offers to rent the Property and negotiating
terms of a lease for the Property. On September 17, 2019, the City Council, excluding Mayor Pro
Tem Haskew who recused herself, by a 4-0 vote, approved an agreement with TRI Commercial
Real Estate ("TRI") to represent the City. Pursuant to the agreement, TRI is serving as the exclusive
listing agent for a period of six (6) months, and will advise the City on various leasing and
marketing issues including market conditions, desirable rent levels, and other tenant improvement
allowances. The City executed a listing agreement with TRI, pursuant to which agrees to pay TRI
a market-rate commission consisting of 6% of the total base rent for the first 60 months in which
rent is to be paid, and 3% of the total base rent for the next 60 months in which rent is to be paid.
Fifty percent of the commission is to be paid upon the mutual execution of a lease by the City and
tenant, and the remaining fifty percent upon the tenant's occupancy and rental commencement. If
the tenant is also represented by a real estate broker, the listing agreement splits the commission
equally between the two brokers.

Mayor Pro Tem Haskew's spouse is a certified public accountant and owns an accounting
business, in which Mayor Pro Tem Haskew holds a community property interest. Within the last
twelve months, Ms. Haskew's spouse provided personal income tax services to the Executive Vice
President ("EVP") of TRI's East Bay division. This particular EVP is part of the team that will be
working with the City pursuant to the City's agreement with TRI. The services provided by Mayor
Pro Tem Haskew's spouse to the EVP included and may include preparing tax returns for the
EVP's personal income and his independently-owned corporation. That independently-owned
corporation is unrelated to TRI and is a separate and distinct entity. Mayor Pro Tem Haskew's
spouse does not provide any accounting services to TRI, and TRI itself has not paid Ms. Haskew's
spouse any money. Mayor Pro Tem Haskew's spouse receives approximately $4,000 each year for
providing income tax services to the EVP, which is less than 1% of his total business receipts. This
fee has been charged and paid on a time and materials basis regardless of changes in the EVP's
income each year. Finally, Mayor Pro Tem Haskew, who is also a certified public accountant, does
not work for or provide services to her spouse's firm. Also, based on confidentiality and
professional/ethical rules applicable to accountants, Mayor Pro Tem Haskew has no access to or
knowledge of the EVP's financial information that her spouse is aware of based on the accounting
services he has provided to the EVP.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act provides, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial

File No. A-19-235
Page No. 3

interest." "A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family," or on certain enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103) Such economic
interests include "[a]ny source of income . . . aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in
value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made." (Section 87103(c).) A public official also has an economic interest in
"[a]ny business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management." (Section 87103(d).)

Regulation 18701(a) provides that "[a] financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to
be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a
governmental decision before the official or the official's agency." Regulation 18701(b) provides
the applicable standard when the economic interest is not explicitly involved in the decision, and
states, "[a] financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if
the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably
foreseeable."

Under Regulation 18702.3, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental
decision on an official's financial interest in a source of income is material if the source is an
individual and "[t]he official knows or has reason to know that the individual has an interest in a
business entity that will be financially affected under the materiality standards in Regulation
18702.1." (Regulation 18702.3(a)(2).) Under Regulation 18702.1, the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's financial interest in a business entity is
material if "[t]he entity is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision . . . ." (Regulation
18702.1(a)(1).)

Here, Mayor Pro Tem Haskew has a source of income interest in the EVP of TRI, due to the
income her spouse receives from the EVP for the accounting and tax services he provides. The EVP
has an economic interest in TRI as a business entity based on the fact that he is "a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management" in the company. (Section
87103(d).) The governmental decisions at issue will have a reasonably foreseeable, material
financial effect on TRI, given that TRI is a named party in, or a subject of, the decisions, having
contracted with the City to act as its agent in the leasing process, and having its compensation tied
directly to the lease. Accordingly, Mayor Pro Tem Haskew is prohibited from taking part in the
decisions.

File No. A-19-235
Page No. 4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:aja

affected by the decisions, given that the company's compensation is directly tied to the leasing 
decisions.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 of the Act provides, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial  
interest." "A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family," or on certain enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103). Such economic

"[a]ny business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, 
or holds any position of management." (Section 87103(d).)

be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
governmental decision before the official or the official's agency." Regulation 18701(b) provides 
the applicable standard when the economic interest is not explicitly involved in the decision, and 
states, "[a] financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if 
the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical, it is reasonably.foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent 
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable." 
Under Regulation 18702.3, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental 
decision on an official's financial interest in a source of income is material if the source is an

financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's financial interest in a business entity is

Here, Mayor Pro Tern Haskew has a source of income interest in the EVP of TRI, due to the 
income her spouse receives from the EVP for the accounting and tax services he provides. The EVP
